coredata.dc:title: Chiral CQD-based PL and CD sensors for high sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Xingang
Jiang, Li
Liu, Yan
Sun, Wei
Qin, Yingxi
Liao, Lei
Qin, Aimiao
coredata.dc:description: Chiral biomas carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great interest because of their low cytotoxicity, low cost and facile synthesis, which are special useful for sensing. In this paper, CQDs derived from cane molasses were endowed chirality by a simple hydrothermal method with l-cysteine and d-cysteine as chiral sources respectively. The obtained chiral CQDs have significantly enhanced fluorescence properties, whose fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield are about 3 times of CQDs without chirality. A fluorescent sensor and a circular dichroic (CD) sensor were built and compared based on the fluorescence property and chirality of the obtained chiral CQDs, respectively, the former exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+, while the latter exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+, Hg2+and Cu2+ simultaneously. The detection mechanism of the CD sensor for metal ions was proposed based on the static quenching mechanism of the fluorescent sensor. The detection of real samples of tap water verified the reliability of the developed sensors. The present work expands the application field of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence spectrum
Circular dichroism
Heavy metal ions
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and chiral CQDs
Practical analysis of real water samples
Morphology and composition of the chiral CQDs
UV–vis absorption and PL properties and stability of chiral CQDs
Practical analysis of real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51564009 , 51468011 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi province (No. 2018JJA160029 ), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices ( 20AA-17 ), Guangxi Graduate Education Innovation Project ( YCB22021062 , YCSW22021199 ), Research Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (No. GUTQDJJ201901 ) and the Science and Technology Base and Talent Special Project of Guangxi Province (No. 21220029 CE ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of synthesis strategy of chiral CQDs.

(a) TEM image of L-CQDs and size distribution of L-CQDs; (b) HRTEM of CQDs, photograph of L-CQDs (c) under white light and (d) under 365 nm UV lamp; (e) FTIR spectra of L-CQDs, CQDs and L-cys; (f) XPS full spectrum of L-CQDs, High resolution XPS spectrum of (g) C1s (h) O1s (i) N1s (j) S2p.
CD spectra of (a) L/D-Cys (b) CQDs and (c) L/D-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (left) and fluorescence excitation spectra (right) of chiral CQDs, CQDs and L/D-Cys; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs, L/D-Cys and chiral CQDs; (c) the fluorescence intensity changes within 30 days and (d) Zeta-potential plots of CQDs and chiral CQDs.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of L-CQDs sensing to different 15 metal ions; (b) the change of the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs within 4 min after adding Ag+ with different concentrations; (c) fluorescence emission spectra of L-CQDs quenched by different concentrations of Ag+ (from 0 to 220μΜ); (d) calibration curves of the degree of fluorescence quenching [(F0–F)/F0] of L-CQDs versus Ag+ concentration.
CD spectra of L-CQDs in the presence of 15 different metal ions.

CD spectra of L-CQDs with different concentrations of (a)Ag+ (from 0 to 140 μM), (b) Hg2+ (0–100 μM) and (c) Cu2+ (from 0 to 100 μM); the calibration Curve CD [mdeg] of L-CQDs versus (d) Ag+ concentration corresponding to (a), (e) Hg2+ concentration corresponding to (b) and (f) Cu2+ concentration corresponding to (c).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (b) time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of L-CQDs in the presence and absence of Ag+; (c) mechanism diagram of the chiral CQDs detecting metal ions by PL sensor(i) and CD sensor(ii).
Detection of Ag+ in actual water samples by L-CQD-based PL sensor.
Detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ in actual water samples by L-CQD-based CD sensor.

head.ce:title: Chiral CQD-based PL and CD sensors for high sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metals & Materials Ministry of Education, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metals & Materials Ministry of Education
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral biomas carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great interest because of their low cytotoxicity, low cost and facile synthesis, which are special useful for sensing. In this paper, CQDs derived from cane molasses were endowed chirality by a simple hydrothermal method with l-cysteine and d-cysteine as chiral sources respectively. The obtained chiral CQDs have significantly enhanced fluorescence properties, whose fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield are about 3 times of CQDs without chirality. A fluorescent sensor and a circular dichroic (CD) sensor were built and compared based on the fluorescence property and chirality of the obtained chiral CQDs, respectively, the former exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+, while the latter exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+, Hg2+and Cu2+ simultaneously. The detection mechanism of the CD sensor for metal ions was proposed based on the static quenching mechanism of the fluorescent sensor. The detection of real samples of tap water verified the reliability of the developed sensors. The present work expands the application field of CQDs.

Cane molasses-based chiral carbon quantum dots (L/D-CQDs) with L/D-Cys as modified agents were successfully produced. The PL and CD sensors based on the L/D-CQDs with enhanced fluorescence performance have great sensitivity and selectivity for detecting heavy metal ions.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is the property of a material entity that has the same structure as its mirror image but cannot overlap completely. For example, our hands are mirror images of one another but are unable to fully overlap [1]. Amino acids are typical chiral compounds made up of four distinct groups linked to a single carbon atom. These molecules are mirror images of one another but are unable to completely overlap one another. Chirality is one of the most basic characteristics of nature. It plays a vital role in the evolution of organisms [2], so it is an important factor in biology, medicine, and chemistry. In the early stage, the research of chirality mainly focused on the molecular field. Since Whetten first found that glutathione nanoclusters have strong optical rotation and further confirmed the existence of chiral nanostructures [3], the research of chiral nanomaterials has attracted more and more researchers, and also plays a core role in chiral separation, chiral detection, and biomedicine [4–6]. As an emerging subclass of luminescent carbonaceous nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be endowed with chiral properties, which making them particularly interesting for sensor applications due to their low cytotoxicity and facile synthesis [7–9].

Preparation methods of CQDs include microwave-assisted method [10], electro chemical method [11], and hydrothermal method [12], and etc. In the previous methods, different precursors have been used to prepared CQDs, such as ethylenediamine, urea, benzoquinone (chemistry), and sucrose. However, the harm that some chemical agents cause to the environment is disregarded. Recently, many natural biomass, such as custard apple peel [13], pelagic Sargassum [14], alkali lignin [15] sugarcane bagasse [16], and rice husks [17], have been chosen as green carbon sources to prepare CQDs due to their Environmentally friendly, resource renewing and low cost. Cane molasses is a polyhydric mixture with a sugar content of 48.0%, which richness in multiple functional groups. More than this, cane molasses is a naturally occurring waste from sugar mills in Guangxi province, and using molasses as a raw material can promote the renewable use of local resources. Chiral carbon quantum dots usually were prepared by one-step synthesis method and two-step synthesis method [18,19]. In the former, chiral CQDs were prepared by a one-pot method in the presence of chiral and carbon sources, while in the latter, CQDs were first made from carbon sources and subsequently modified with chiral sources. The latter technique has the benefits of being simple to modify and performing well. For example, Li et al. [19] prepared chiral CQDs by using citric acid as the carbon source to synthesize carbon nuclei and a chiral amino acid precursor L/d-cysteine (L- or d-cysteine) as the modifier, and the addition of the chiral source greatly improved the emission quantum yield of CQDs. L/d-cysteine is common chiral precursor that is not only abundant in amino and carboxyl groups, but also inexpensive and simple to obtain. Therefore, using L/d-cysteine and cane molasses as raw materials offers the possibility to synthesize chiral CQDs with excellent properties.

Chiral CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties can be used as fluorescence sensors for detection of metal ions. Gao et al. [20] synthesized L-CQDs with citric acid and l-aspartic acid as raw materials, the fluorescence of L-CQDs could be quenched by Sn2+ and restored by l-lysine, the fluorescence sensor is constructed to detect Sn2+ and l-lysine. Liang et al. [21] modified carbon dots with new imidazole to detect nickel ions (Ni2+), the LOD is 0.93 mM. The fluorescence sense platform based on imidazole modified CQDs can be applied to Ni2+ detection in environment. Although chiral CQDs have made encouraging progress in detection, the high detection limit and simultaneous detection of multiple metal ions are still challenging problems. Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, an optical rotation spectrum is often used to infer the configuration and conformation of asymmetric molecules. For example, Koenis et al. [22] showed how vibrational CD spectra was utilized to distinguish between mechanical stereoisomers as well as give precise details on their conformations. Recently CD spectrum has become a valuable tool in studying the chirality of nanomaterials [23–25]. Xiao et al. [26] designed and synthesized a highly fluorinated molecular probe made of salicylaldehyde derivatives to detect chiral amines in the fluorine phase by CD spectra. Tedsana et al. [27] proposed a new CD sensor for detecting Ni2+ and Co2+ based on chiral cadmium sulfide quantum dots. Additionally, the intensity of the CD spectrum grew linearly as a function of Ni2+ or Co2+ concentration, which might be helpful for the quantitative analysis.

So far, chiral CQDs have been applied in fluorescence sensor [28–30], but rarely reported in CD sensor. In this work, chiral CQDs were prepared by a two-step hydrothermal method with cane molasses and L/d-cysteine as chiral source and chiral precursor respectively. The obtained chiral CQDs have significantly enhanced fluorescence properties. Based on the fluorescent and chiral properties of CQDs, a fluorescent detection method and CD detection method were constructed and compared respectively. Moreover, the detection mechanism of the two methods was also analyzed.

Cane molasses was provided by Ganhua Group Co., Ltd., Guigang, Guangxi, China. L/d-cysteine(L/D-cys) was purchased from Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Analytical grade AgCl, AlCl3, BaCl2, FeCl3, CdCl2, Co(NO3)2, CrCl3, CuCl2, FeCl3, Hg(NO3)2, MgCl2, MnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2 and (CH3COO)2Pb were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd, Guangdong, China. All reagents were analytical grade and were used strictly according to regulation in our works. The water used in all experiments was ultrapure water (UP water).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and chiral CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, primary CQDs and chiral CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1 . Cane molasses (4.5 mL) was added into 70.0 mL of ultrapure water and dispersed with ultrasonic for 10 min. Then the obtained homogeneous aqueous solution was transferred to a 100 mL steel high-pressure reactor and heated in a hot-air oven at 160 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained carbon slag residue was separated from the initial product by filtration using a 0.2 μm filter paper to acquire CQDs primary liquid. 0.4 g L/d-cysteine was added in the CQDs primary liquid (10 mL) and mixed with ultrasonic for 10 min, then transferred to a 25 mL steel high-pressure reactor and heated in a hot-air oven at 120 °C for 2 h. The next steps are the similar to the proceed of the CQDs primary liquid, the difference is that the obtained filtrate was further centrifuged (10,000 r) for 10 min to acquire chiral CQDs primary liquid. Here, the chiral CQDs with l-cysteine and d-cysteine as chiral source were named as L-CQDs and D-CQDs, respectively. The CQDs and chiral CQDs primary liquid were diluted to 100 times with UP water to produce the CQDs and chiral CQDs solution for fluorescence and UV–vis spectra tests.

Various physicochemical techniques were used to determine the surface morphology, chemical component, and optical properties of the synthesized CQDs and chiral CQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was executed on a JEM-2100F TEM at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was collected using a Thermo Nexus 470FT-IR spectrometer (KBr disk). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using ESCALAB250Xi. The fluorescence lifetime of the synthetic samples was collected using an QuantaMaster 8000. Circular dichroism was performed on a JASCO, J-1500CD spectro-Polarimeter, with a scanning rate at 200 nm/min. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded by a UV-3600 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer and a VARIAN Cary 100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (the voltage is set to 580 V and the slit widths of excitation and emission are both set to 5 nm). The ζ-potential of CQDs and chiral CQDs were collected on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (ZS 90, UK).

The fluorescence QY of the synthesized CQDs and chiral CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate, which was dissolved in a 0.1 M aqueous solution of sulfuric acid (QY = 55%), as a reference. In reducing the reabsorption effect, the absorption value of the formulated solution was generally <0.05 (absorption at 350 nm). The fluorescence QY of the synthetic chiral CQDs can be calculated based on the following equation:
β = βS × I/IS × AS/A × η2/ηS 2
where β represents the fluorescence QY, I is the measured fluorescence integral area at 350 nm excitation, η shows the refractive index of the solvent, A denotes the absorbance, and subscript s is a known fluorescent standard substance.

In the qualitative detection of metal ions, 100 μL of chiral CQDs primary liquid and 200 μL of metal ions (Cr3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, and Ni2+) solutions (0.01 M) were mixed and diluted to 10 mL with UP water, respectively. 4 mL of diluted solution was put into quartz cuvette and let it stand for 2 min before detection.

To detect metal ions quantitatively, 100 μL of chiral CQDs primary liquid was firstly diluted to 2 mL with UP water, and then different concentrations of Ag+ ions were added to the above solution before diluting the volume to 10 mL with UP water. Finally, the PL spectra of the mixed solution with excited wavelength of 350 nm were recorded.

To study the circular dichroism spectra of the synthesized chiral CQDs, 10 μL of chiral CQDs primary liquid was diluted to 10 mL with UP water. To study the circular dichroism spectra of the synthesized chiral CQDs, the following procedure was carried out. 10 μL of chiral CQDs primary liquid was mixed with the individual stock solution of metal ions including Cr3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, and Ni2+ solutions (0.01 M) to obtain the final concentration of 100 μM. Fill a quartz cuvette with 4 mL of the combined solution, and wait 2 min before detecting it.

To detect metal ions quantitatively, after diluting 10 μL of chiral CQDs primary liquid with UP water to 2 mL, different concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ ions were added to the aforementioned solution separately. The volume was then diluted to 10 mL with UP water. Finally, CD spectra of the mixed solution were recorded at 249 nm, 250 nm, and 204 nm for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical analysis of real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the reliability of the chiral CQDs based PL and CD sensors, we used the tap water from Guilin University of Technology for test with the standard addition method. By measuring various concentrations (50 μM and 100 μM) of Ag+ in spiked sample, the proposed PL sensor's accuracy and precision were assessed. Similarly, the accuracy and precision of the proposed CD sensor were evaluated by measuring different concentrations (50 μM and 100 μM) of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ in spiked sample. To guarantee the correctness of the results, sample concentrations were determined using the standard curve five times for each test. The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were then calculated using the average of these five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition of the chiral CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of chiral CQDs is shown in Fig. 1. TEM images of L-CQDs and CQDs show that L-CQDs are almost spherical and have a uniform distribution (Fig. 2 a), which is similar to that of CQDs (Fig. S1(a)) and D-CQDs (Fig. S2a). The particle size of L-CQDs obtained by measuring 100 particles is mainly distributed between 1.5 nm and 6.5 nm with an average diameter of 3.6 nm (illustration of Fig. 2a). High-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM) is shown in Fig. 2b, the lattice spacing of L-CQDs is 0.31 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. The particle size of CQDs (illustrated in Fig. S1a) is mainly distributed between 0.5 nm and 2.8 nm with an average particle size of 1.7 nm, and HRTEM image shows that the lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.23 nm (Fig. S1b). The morphology and structure of L-CQDs is similar to that of D-CQDs (Figs. S2a and S2b), but the particle size of chiral CQDs is larger than that of CQDs, which may be caused by the modification of cysteine. The solution of chiral CQDs appears light yellow under sunlight (Fig. 2c), and emits blue fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 2d).

The FT-IR spectra were collected to explore functional groups on the surface of chiral CQDs. Fig. 2e displays the FT-IR spectra of L-Cys, CQDs, and L-CQDs. The broadbands in 3456 cm−1, 1759 cm−1, and 1399 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H, CO and C–O, respectively [31]. Compared to the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, the FT-IR spectrum of L-CQDs shows two additional peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1, which are caused by the stretching vibration, the bending vibration of N–H, and the stretching vibration of –CH2-, respectively [32]. Two additional peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of L-CQDs are similar to the L-Cys, which demonstrates that the L-Cys have been modified on the CQDs. The FT-IR spectrum of D-CQDs (shown in Fig. S2c) is similar to that of L-CQDs, indicating their similar optical performance.

The chemical components of chiral CQDs were characterized by XPS. Fig. 2f shows the full XPS spectrum of L-CQDs, which indicates that there are C, O, N, and S elements in L-CQDs. The graphitic sp2 carbon structure of the L-CQDs, which has the highest binding energy peak at 284.7 eV, is exhibited in the high-resolution spectra of C1s in Fig. 2g, demonstrating the presence of C–C. The peak at 285.7 eV demonstrates the presence of C–O, C–S, and C–N. In addition, the peak at 288.2 eV should be assigned to CO groups [33,34]. In Fig. 2h, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s can be divided into two peaks, the peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.5 eV demonstrate the presence of C–O and CO respectively. The C–N group peak in 400.7 eV and the N–H group peak in 401.9 eV are induced by the modification of L-Cys, and the peak at 404 eV attributed to π-excitations is caused by charging effects [35], according to the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2i). Fig. 2j displays the high-resolution S2p spectrum of L-CQDs, in which the peaks at 163.4 eV and 164.7 eV are correspond to sulfhydryl (S–H) [36,37] and S–C [38] covalent bonds on L-Cys. Fig. S1c shows the full XPS spectrum of CQDs, which can be concluded that to contains elements N and C. Fig. S1d describes the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, in which two peaks correspond to CC (284.8 eV) and CO (286.3 eV) groups [39]. Fig. S1e shows the high- resolution spectrum of O1s, in which two peaks correspond to C–O (532.1 eV) and CO (533.1 eV) groups [40]. Compared with L-CQDs, N and S elements are missing in the XPS spectrum of CQDs, which may be caused by the lack of L-Cys modification. The XPS spectrum of D-CQDs is similar to that of L-CQDs (Figs. S2d–h), indicating the functional groups on the surface of the L-CQDs and D-CQDs are the same.

To explore the chiral properties of the synthesis chiral CQDs, CD spectra of the L/D-CQDs, L/D-Cys, and CQDs were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 a, it can be seen that the CD signals of L/D-Cys are present at 201 nm, which is the inherent chiral signal of L/D-Cys. For CQDs, it is almost a straight line in Fig. 3b, indicating that they have no chiral signal. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, opposite cotton effects of L/D-CQDs are observed in the CD spectra in the wavelength range of 200 nm–300 nm [41], in addition to a CD signal at 204 nm from the inheritance of L/D-Cys, another signal emerges at 253 nm, which may be derived from the π-π* conjugation of the sp2-hybridized carbon network in L/D-CQDs [26,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and PL properties and stability of chiral CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of L/D-CQDs, CQDs and L/D-Cys are shown in Fig. 4 a(left). For the L/D-CQDs, three absorption peaks at 206 nm, 276 nm, and 350 nm are observed. The peak at 276 nm can be assigned to the π-π* transition of the CC band [43], and the peak at 350 nm should be assigned to n-π* transition of the CO band [44], which is caused by surface carboxyl amino or other moieties that passivate the surface traps. In addition, the strong absorption peak at 210 nm is due to the preservation of L/D-Cys precursor compared to the UV–vis absorption spectrum of pure L/D-Cys. For the CQDs, an absorption peak at 250 nm is observed, which can be assigned to the π-π* transition of the CC band [45].

The fluorescence spectra of the chiral CQDs are shown in Fig. 4a,b. Compared to CQDs without chirality and pure L/D-Cys, the chiral CQDs shows an enhanced fluorescence property and emit higher indigo fluorescence than CQDs under the irradiation of UV lamp (illustrated in the inset of Fig. 4b). The maximum fluorescence intensity of L/D-CQDs excited at 350 nm of wavelengths is about three times that of CQDs (Fig. 4b), and L-CQDs show slightly higher fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield than D-CQDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of L-CQDs and D-CQDs is 18.86% and 17.38%, respectively, which are also about three times that of CQDs (6.39%). Compared with the previous report by Huang et al. [46] that the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs synthesized from molasses was 5.8%, the obtained chiral CQDs in this work have greatly improved their fluorescence quantum yield.

In Figs. S3(a and b), the relationship between the fluorescence emission peak position and the excitation wavelength of the obtained chiral CQDs was investigated, by exciting different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 370 nm in an interval of 10 nm, as shown in Fig. S3. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission spectra exhibit a red shift phenomenon similar to those previously reported in the literature [47]. Moreover, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission intensity first increases and then decreases, showing a typical excitation wavelength dependence.

Fig. 4c shows the change of fluorescence intensity of the chiral CQDs and CQDs without chirality within 30 days, and it is found that the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CQDs and CQDs decreases with time, but the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CQDs decreases more slowly than that of CQDs, which indicates that the chiral CQDs have greater stability than CQDs. The Zeta-potential plot in Fig. 4d shows that chiral CQDs have a strong peak near 10 mV in the negative electric region and a weak peak near 26 mV in the positive electric region, while the CQDs display only one peak near 4 mV in the negative electric region. In terms of absolute value, the chiral CQDs clearly have a higher potential than the negatively charged CQDs, which further demonstrates the stability of chiral CQDs.

The fluorescence property of chiral CQDs promises the feasibility of an application. Here, to develop a fluorescence sensor for metal ions sensing, the fluorescence emission spectra of chiral CQDs homogeneous solutions containing 200 μM of various single-metal ions (Cr3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, and Ni2+) were scanned with a 350 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. The (F/F0) bar diagram of the chiral CQDs with the above ions is shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S4a, where F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5a and Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CQDs is quenched only by Ag+. Other metal ions have almost no any interference with the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CQDs. Therefore, a fluorescence sensor based on the chiral CQDs shows a potential application in higher selectivity for Ag+ detection. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence intensity of the L-CQDs solution changes over time after adding 60 μM or 100 μM of Ag+, respectively. The fact that the fluorescence intensity first drops and then tends to stabilize over time, essentially achieving stability after 2 min, suggests that the quenching can be finished in that amount of time. Therefore, we chose 2 min as the appropriate standing time during the test. To discuss the sensitivity of chiral CQDs as fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection, the emission spectra of chiral CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Ag+ were recorded and shown in Fig. 5c and Fig. S4b. As shown in Fig. 5d, the quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) of L-CQDs displays a good linear response to the concentration of Ag+ in the range of 40–220 μM, the linear equation was calculated to be (F0–F)/F0 = −0.00495 + 0.00172 [Ag+] (R2 = 0.99), the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 3.38 μM by the 3δ/m (δ is the standard deviation of the lowest signal and m is the slope of linear calibration plot). In Fig. S4c, D-CQDs displays a similar response to the concentration of Ag+ in the range of 20–140 μM, the linear equation and LOD were calculated to be (F0–F)/F0 = −0.0556 + 0.00142 [Ag+] (R2 = 0.99), and 4.09 μM, respectively. It can be concluded that in the detection of Ag+, L-CQDs show slightly higher sensitivity than D-CQDs. A comparison of the sensor based on chiral CQDs in this work with other fluorescent sensors for the detection of Ag+ is presented in Table S1, demonstrating that the proposed sensor in our work has a lower detection limit for Ag+, and a potential application of chiral CQDs for the sensitive and selective detection of Ag+ in aqueous solution.

We have monitored the changes in the CD spectra of chiral CQDs after adding various single-metal ions (Cr3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, and Ni2+) respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S5. It can be seen that the CD spectra of chiral CQDs significantly change only in the presence of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. When just Ag+ is present, three peaks are seen at 210 nm, 249 nm, and 282 nm. The 210 nm peak enhances the chiral peak of the chiral CQDs, whereas the 249 nm and 282 nm peaks indicate opposing chiral signals from the chiral CQDs. Similarly, three peaks are also seen in CD spectra when Hg2+ is the only ion present, but the peak at 219 nm exhibits the reverse chiral signal while the peaks at 204 nm and 250 nm enhance the chiral peak of the chiral CQDs. When Cu2+ is the lone component, only a peak at 204 nm is observed and reduces the chiral peak of the chiral CQDs. Except for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, when other ions were introduced individually, the peak at 204 nm from the intrinsic chiral peak of chiral CQDs has no any change, showing that the other ions had no effect on the chiral signal of L-CQDs. These findings imply that qualitative and quantitative analyses of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ may be performed using the CD sensor based on the chiral CQDs.

The quantitative analysis based on the CD spectra of the chiral CQDs was then studied by constructing the calibration curve for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+. Under optimal conditions, the standard solution of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ was titrated into the chiral CQDs solution in the proper volume, respectively. From Fig. 7 a–c, it can be seen that the CD signal of L-CQDs at the wavelength of 249 nm, 250 and 204 nm changes linearly with the increasing concentration of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively, the CD signal D-CQDs is similar to that of L-CQDs (From Figs. S6a–c). Plotting the CD signal (mdeg) as a function of the concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ allows for the creation of the calibration curve for these three ions. As shown in Fig. 7d–f, the working curves are linear over the concentration range of 20–140 μΜ, 20–100 μΜ and 20–100 μΜ, for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+respectively, the corresponding regression equations are CD = −0.5309-0.1012 [Ag+] (μΜ) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9982, CD = −0.7272-0.0571 [Hg2+] (μΜ) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9985 and CD = 14.021-0.0612 [Cu2+] (μΜ) with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9690, respectively. And the corresponding LODs are calculated to be 0.39 μM (Ag+), 0.68 μM (Hg2+) and 0.64 μM (Cu2+), respectively. As shown in Figs. S6d–f, D-CQDs show a similar behavior to the L-CQDs for detecting Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, which have the same linear range and almost the same LOD. The related regression equations are CD = −0.9211 + 0.171 [Ag+] (μΜ), CD = 0.5269-0.0356 [Hg2+] (μΜ) and CD = 13.703-0.0646 [Cu2+] (μΜ), respectively, with correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9975, 0.9981, and 0.9919 and the LODs of 0.31 μM, 0.64 μM, and 0.61 μM, respectively.

Table S2 shows the comparison of the CD sensors based on chiral CQDs in this work with other developed methods for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection, it is shown that the proposed sensors in this work have a widely linear rang and much lower LOD for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ detection. Interestingly, it is found that the LOD for Ag+ detection is much lower than that of the PL sensor in this work, indicating that the CD sensor constructed by chiral CQDs is more sensitive for Ag+ detection in comparison to the PL sensor and has attractive application prospects.

In order to study the detection mechanism of the proposed chiral CQDs sensors, we took L-CQDs as an illustration. Firstly, the fluorescence lifetimes and UV–vis spectra were used to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the chiral CQDs by metal ions. The UV–vis spectra of the L-CQDs with and without Ag+ are shown in Fig. 8 a. Compared to the L-CQDs without Ag+, the absorption peaks of the L-CQDs in the presence of Ag+ at 280 nm and 350 nm exhibit a little red shift and diminish. The reason may be that the Ag+ ions combine with carboxyl/hydroxyl groups on the surface of L-CQDs and form a complex, which modifies the configuration of L-CQDs. The fluorescence lifetimes of the L-CQDs in the absence and in presence of Ag+ with concentrations of 50 μM, 100 μM and 150 μM are also shown in Fig. 8b and Table S3. Compared to the L-CQDs without Ag+, the weighted average fluorescence lifetimes calculated from the fluorescence decay curves of L-CQDs-Ag+(50–150 μM) systems have hardly changed. Therefore, it can be inferred from the results of the examination of the UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetimes that Ag+ can mix with L-CQDs to form a complex. The radiation transition mechanism by which excited electrons return to the ground state can be hampered by this combination, which leads to the quenching fluorescence and conforms to the static quenching mechanism [48,49]. The diagram of quenching fluorescence mechanism of chiral CQDs for detecting Ag+ and corresponding energy level diagrams are described in Fig. 8c(i).

Based on the analysis of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the chiral CQDs by Ag+, we can also propose the possible mechanism of CD sensor for detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, that is, metel ions(Ag+, Hg2+ or Cu2+) can combine with the chiral carbon atoms and form coordination bonds, which leads to the changes of the spatial arrangement of the chiral carbon atoms and causes the chirality of the nanomaterial to vary [27,50], so different signals can be seen on the CD spectra when Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ are present in the chiral CQDs solution, the diagram of the mechanism is shown in Fig. 8c, ii. CQDs, since they connect to the.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical analysis of real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted experiments based on the standard added method to demonstrate the feasibility of the PL and CD sensors in actual samples. Different concentrations of Ag+ were added to the water samples, we can see from Table 1 that the recoveries of Ag+ are within the range of 97.7–101.8% (RSD <2.88%) for tap water. Similarly, different concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ were respectively added to the water sample to test the CD spectra. Table 2 demonstrates that the Ag+ recoveries fall within the range of 99.1–102.5% (RSD <2.68%) for tap water, the recoveries of Hg2+ were within the range of 98.3–102.5% (RSD <3.15%) and the recoveries of Cu2+ were within the range of 100.4–101.5% (RSD <2.67%). The RSD of each sample is less than 5%, demonstrating that the analysis recoveries are desirably satisfactory and the developed sensors can possess great potential in the practical applications of probing heavy metal ions in environmental water samples.

In summary, blue fluorescent chiral CQDs were smoothly synthesized using cane molasses as carbon source and L/D-Cys as chiral agent by a two-step hydrothermal method. The obtained chiral CQDs display a spheric morphology with uniform size and good dispersion, along with greatly enhanced fluorescent performance and stability compared to CQDs without chirality. Combining their chiral and fluorescence properties, the chiral CQDs can be utilized directly as a fluorescent probe and a CD sensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of multiple heavy metal ions. Although there are still many challenges with chiral CQDs, with the deepening of research, their chiral and fluorescent properties will bring more progress in detection and chiral recognition.

Xingang Fan: performed the experiments, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, the manuscript with input from all authors. Li Jiang: participated in study design and manuscript revision. Yan Liu: participated in the experiments, data compilation and, Formal analysis. Wei Sun: provided suggestions for improving the experiment. Yingxi Qin: provided suggestions for improving the experiment. Lei Liao: participated in study design and manuscript revision. Aimiao Qin: Conceptualization, and designed the study, provided, Resources, data, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China(No.51564009, 51468011), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi province (No.2018JJA160029), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Materials and Devices (20AA-17), Guangxi Graduate Education Innovation Project (YCB22021062, YCSW22021199), Research Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (No.GUTQDJJ201901) and the Science and Technology Base and Talent Special Project of Guangxi Province (No. 21220029 CE).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

SUPPORTING INFORMATIONThe following Supporting Information is available free of charge.Comparison of some PL sensors based on CQDs for Ag+ detection; comparison of the proposed CD sensor with other sensors for the detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+; fluorescence Decay Time of CQDs, L-CQDs and L-CQDs-Ag+; TEM image and size distribution of CQDs, HRTEM image of CQDs, XPS full and high resolution spectra of CQDs; TEM image and size distribution of D-CQDs, HRTEM image of D-CQDs, FTIR spectrum of D-CQDs, XPS full and high resolution spectra of D-CQDs; fluorescence emission spectra of L/D-CQDs recorded at progressively longer excitation wavelengths; the fluorescence spectra of D-CQDs sensing to different 15 metal ions, fluorescence emission spectra of D-CQDs quenched by different concentrations of Ag+, calibration curves of the degree of fluorescence quenching [(F0–F)/F0] of D-CQDs versus Ag+ concentration; CD spectra of D-CQDs in the presence of 15 different metal ions; CD spectra of D-CQDs change with different concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, the corresponding calibration Curve CD of D-CQDs versus the concentration of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+.
